{{Chess Opening Theory/Position|= |Scandinavian Defence| |rd|nd|bd|qd|kd|bd|nd|rd|= |pd|pd|pd| |pd|pd|pd|pd|= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | | | | | | | |= | | |nl| | | | | |= |pl|pl|pl|pl| |pl|pl|pl|= |rl| |bl|ql|kl|bl|nl|rl|= || }} = Scandinavian Defense: Valencian Variation = The retreat with 3...Qd8 was depicted in Castellvi–Vinyoles: the '''Valencian Variation,'''{{Cite journal|title=Revista de ajedrez "Peón de Rey": La Variante Valenciana de la Defensa Escandinava|page=[https://www.peonderey.com/project/pdr-162/ 80]|year=2023|publisher=Ed. EDAMI|location=Barcelona|last=N. de Arenas, S. and Rubio, F.|url=https://www.peonderey.com/project/pdr-162/}} the oldest of all Scandinavian lines. Prior to the 20th century, it was often considered the main line, and was characterized as "best" by [[Howard Staunton]]''.''{{Cite journal|title=The chess-player's handbook: A popular and scientific introduction to the game of chess|page=[https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_fd8CAAAAYAAJ/page/n262 377]|year=1864|publisher=Henry G. Bohn|location=London|last=Staunton|first=Howard|url=https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_fd8CAAAAYAAJ}} In the 1960s, 3...Qd8 experienced something of a revival after the move was played in a game by Bronstein against GM [[Andrija Fuderer]] in 1959.Harman and Taulbut, p.68 Bronstein's game featured the older line 4.d4 Nf6, while other grandmasters, including [[Karl Robatsch]], explored fianchetto systems with 4.d4 g6 and a later ...Ng8–h6. The 3...Qd8 variation as a whole remains playable, although it is now considered somewhat passive. It is played particularly by IMs [[John Bartholomew (American chess player)|John Bartholomew]] and Daniel Lowinger, and by the GMs David Garcia Ilundain and Nikola Djukic. ==Theory table== {{ChessTable}}. 1. e4 d5 2. exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qd8
